I wrote about Nazis report-brigading on Twitter a year ago and it's still happening today https://t.co/SwGJMfJhb6

it's just hilarious to me that we have product managers basically designing society and a justice system and there are experts at things like that and they do NOT know how to code or make jira tickets
imagining a Twitter stakeholder meeting but Twitter is a co-owned by all it's users and it's just a massive general assembly of all interested users held in a football stadium, it lasts 36 weeks
call me in 200 years when we have a Twitter Supreme Court and I'll just laugh and laugh and laugh

the way to generate rulesets that are to your users liking is generally to ASK THEM AND THEN LISTEN instead of sitting in your fortress in the tenderloin and justifying doing nothing for like a decade
if only someone had thought really deeply about online governance structures *checks notes* 20 years ago https://t.co/ZQtT94YDpc https://t.co/DXoR55gSGQ
fire the product managers and hire community managers, is all I'm saying

Community experts: "The reporting feature is ripe for abuse and the right-wing are organizing report brigades on Telegram to target people they don't like!"

Twitter: "What about un-vetted community panels to fact-check tweets by majority?"

I have a lot of earnest questions about what has held Twitter back from doing a better job of community management. I have heard of warring project managers and no cohesive product vision, the inherent problems of working at a truly massive scale, but none fully explain it imo

It is becoming clearer that Twitter is filled with well-paid employees who justify their salaries by refusing to do even the most basic things that community experts are begging them to do and who simply invent things no one wants to under-solve the issues https://t.co/8KchNuPHea
